result:
The first thing that strikes me is how 1,000 words is both a cage and a key. I could spend them describing the tremor in my great-aunt’s left hand the year she forgot my name, or I could try to name every shade of blue I’ve seen in an ocean I’ve never touched. The truth is, I’m terrified of finishing this exercise, because once the word counter hits zero I’ll have to decide whether any of it mattered. Still, terror is just another shade of curiosity, so let’s keep going.

I keep returning to the summer I was eleven, when my best friend and I built a raft out of discarded milk jugs we found behind the Sadler’s dairy. We lashed them with baling wire because rope sounded too official, too permanent. We named the raft “Maybe Tomorrow” and launched it into the cow pond, expecting it to carry us like pilgrims toward the center where the water went black and cold. It sank in under a minute, folding us into pond stink and laughter. That night I wrote in my journal: “We drowned the raft, but not the dream.” I underlined dream three times, the pencil grooves so deep the next two pages carried phantom bulges, like Braille only I could read. I wonder if memory is just the residue of indentations we forgot we made.

Lately I’ve been watching a neighbor’s oak produce leaves that behave like birds: they lift off in unison, swirl skyward, then settle back onto the same branches, a green murmuration. I stand at the window with coffee cooling in my hand, trying to decide whether witnessing something impossible makes it less impossible, or whether the world is simply ratcheting up its weirdness budget because we’ve all grown bored with ordinary miracles. Either way, the oak doesn’t charge admission, so I keep showing up. Yesterday a single leaf broke formation and drifted across the yard to tap against my kitchen pane, a Morse code I almost remembered from a life before alphabets. I tapped back: “Tell me how to stay astonished.” The leaf didn’t answer, but the silence felt prepositional, like it was leading somewhere.

Inside my desk drawer sits a small tin that once held mints. Now it stores buttons I’ve harvested from clothes that no longer exist: the burgundy toggle from my grandfather’s overcoat, a pearl disc from my mother’s wedding dress, a cracked brown button off my high-school corduroys that still smells faintly of garage-band rehearsals and cheap cologne. Sometimes I dump them onto the blotter and arrange them like planets. The burgundy one always gets to be Jupiter; it bullies the others into orbits, pretending gravity is a choice. I’ve started naming the moons—Regret, Continuance, Maybe If—and I watch them circle until the sun sets and the brass lamp burns their colors down to silhouettes. When I scoop them back into the tin, they clink like tiny bells, as if object permanence were something to celebrate rather than mourn.

There’s a theory that the universe is expanding because every lie we tell adds another layer of dark energy. I heard it from a cab driver in Chicago who also claimed he once gave a ride to a younger version of himself who forgot the fare. I tipped extra, just in case causality was listening. Since then I’ve tried to keep my lies petite: “I’m on my way” when I haven’t found shoes, “I never think about you anymore” when I’m building cathedrals from the echo of your laugh. I figure small lies are like diet soda—artificially sweet, but unlikely to rip new wormholes. Still, I sometimes picture all those cumulative fibs ballooning on the cosmic scale, nudging galaxies farther apart, isolating us one from another until every sky goes dark. If that’s true, then love letters are counterweights, ballast stitched of candor and longing. So tonight I’m writing one to no one in particular: “I miss you even though we’ve never met. I miss the inside jokes we haven’t had time to outgrow, the coffee rings on unfinished maps, the way your snore probably sounds like a small airplane learning to yodel. Please find me before entropy does.” I read it aloud and the room stays the same, but somewhere a star chooses not to die.

Have you ever noticed how bridges breathe? Stand mid-span on a windy evening and feel the subtle lift, like the deck is inhaling on behalf of everyone too busy to notice. I crossed one tonight—old steel, green paint flaking like dried herbs—and halfway over I felt the inhale, then the long exhale that smelled faintly of river and rust. I thought: this is what it’s like to be a syllable inside the sentence of a city, a consonant clacking against the vowel of water. The bridge didn’t ask where I was going; bridges understand ambiguity better than priests. Halfway across I nearly dropped my keys, and the sound they made skittering toward the railing was a plosive, a hard k- sound that rhymed with keep, with cure, with kite. I imagined them slipping through the grate, down into the black seam of river, and felt no panic—only the possibility that somewhere downstream a kid with a magnet and a stick would reel them in, attach them to a brand-new life that might include locking the door behind him when he leaves for good, keys jangling like tiny possibilities.

Time is supposed to be the thing that keeps everything from happening at once, but lately it feels more like a lazy librarian who shoves all the books on one shelf and wonders why the stories hook elbows and start dancing. Last week I heard a recording of my future self leaving a voicemail: “Don’t open the blue envelope until you’ve learned to forgive the color indigo.” The line went dead before I could ask whether forgiveness is transitive, whether I could forgive on behalf of someone else and clear the cosmic ledger that way. I don’t even have a blue envelope, so I’ve started checking every mailbox I pass, nodding politely at strangers as if to say, “I’m only mildly deranged, nothing to worry about.” Maybe tomorrow I’ll buy indigo socks and burn them in effigy, or maybe I’ll plant them in soil to see whether forgiveness grows on nylon. Either way, the future can leave another voicemail; I’m busy learning how to breathe underwater.

Because that’s the trick nobody mentions: we are all breathing underwater. The air we gulp is an ocean of gases, heavy with exhalations of extinct species, rust particles from shuttered factories, the last sighs of dying stars. Every lungful is a museum, a mobile aquarium. I picture the molecules queueing up like tourists: a nitrogen atom that once belonged to a tyrannosaurus’s roar, an oxygen whisper from Shakespeare’s last fever, a trace of argon that danced in the auroras of a planet we haven’t discovered yet. We breathe their stories and exhale our own, trading epics in the dark of the chest, an economy older than money. No wonder a sigh feels like relief and grief at once; it’s history leaving the body, making room for the next unknowable stanza.

I have 147 words left, and I still haven’t told you about the woman who folds paper boats out of parking tickets and launches them into storm drains, convinced they’ll reach the sea; or the boy on the bus who counts prime numbers in Polish because it keeps the wolves of memory at bay; or the dream where I am a library and you are the match, and we both burn happily ever after. I wanted to describe how night tastes like cold iron and overripe peaches, how dawn sometimes forgets her lines and improvises with lavender haze, how my heartbeat sounds like a typewriter that refuses to quit even though the war ended decades ago. I wanted, I wanted. But the counter dwindles, and wanting is just another word for bridge, for breath, for the small metal sound buttons make when they swear they’ll never forget.

So here’s the last sentence, stretching its arms to fit whatever you bring: May every word you lose find its way home, May every word you spend come back as light—

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
